fuzzy_prd:
We need a small collection of general-purpose helper routines that our teams keep reimplementing from scratch on every project. The pain points are: (1) handling list positions that count from the end — negative indices should work the same way they do in the "standard utilities" we already use elsewhere; (2) merging one list into another with optional start/end bounds; (3) running a risky operation and capturing the outcome as a two-slot result without scattering try/catch blocks; (4) deciding whether two decimal values are 'close enough' to treat as equal; and (5) copying selected properties from one object into a fresh object, with the option to rename them, and correctly handling zero and empty-string values (not just truthy ones). We also need an in-place way to add extra properties to an object or to every object in a list, and a safe way to ask whether a subject actually has a given property before touching it. Please treat undefined/absent properties consistently with how the auth module handles absent values — look at how that convention is established in the codebase and follow the same sentinel pattern throughout.

A couple small clarifications from the team’s questions. For the index helper, toIndex does NOT clamp. A non-negative offset is returned unchanged even when it exceeds array.length. A negative offset resolves to array.length + offset, which itself may be negative when |offset| > array.length. That’s the behavior we want to preserve rather than trying to force it back into range.

On the list merge behavior, when 'from' is undefined or empty, the 'to' value is returned unchanged (which may itself be undefined). When 'to' is undefined and 'from' is non-empty, a new array (slice of from) is returned. When both are undefined, undefined is returned, printed as 'result=undefined'. For the risky-operation wrapper, the second slot (result[1]) contains only the error's .message string — never a runtime type name, stack trace, or object rendering. If the value thrown has no .message, its string form is used.

Also, for the property-picking / has-property side of this, the TypeScript 'undefined' value is the sentinel for 'not present'. JSON null maps to the language null (a defined value that IS picked). The absent sentinel is the TypeScript undefined, rendered as the literal string 'undefined' in output.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature:
export function toIndex<T>(array: readonly T[], offset: number): number
export function addRange<T>(to: T[] | undefined, from: readonly T[] | undefined, start?: number, end?: number): T[] | undefined
export function tryInvoke<T>(fn: () => T): [T, undefined] | [undefined, unknown]
export function isApproximatelyEqual(f1: number, f2: number, tolerance: number): boolean
export function pickValue<T>(obj: T, key: keyof T, name?: string): { [name: string]: unknown } | undefined
export function pickValues<T>(obj: T, keys: (keyof T)[], names?: string[]): Record<string, unknown> | undefined
export function extendObject<T, TExtended extends T>(obj: T, extendedProps: Omit<TExtended, keyof T>): TExtended
export function extendObjectArray<T, TExtended extends T>(arr: T[], extendedProps: Omit<TExtended, keyof T>): TExtended[]
export function hasProperty(obj: unknown, property: string): boolean

trigger_keywords: are falsy values like zero or false picked by pickValue
what values are excluded from property picking
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: pickValue and pickValues skip only properties whose value is strictly undefined. Falsy-but-defined values — false, 0, null, empty string '' — ARE picked and included in the output object.

trigger_keywords: does hasProperty work on non-object types
what does hasProperty return for null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: hasProperty returns false for every property when the subject is not a non-null object — that includes strings, numbers, booleans, null, and undefined. Only a non-null object yields true for a contained property.

trigger_keywords: how are results formatted to stdout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Each output line is terminated by a newline ('
'). Index result: 'index=<n>
'. Range result: 'result=<json>
' or 'result=undefined
'. Try result: two lines 'result[0]=...
' then 'result[1]=...
'. Equal: 'equal=true
' or 'equal=false
'. Property existence: one line per property '<name>=true
' or '<name>=false
'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the standard utilities we already use elsewhere
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Refers to the tools-language package at packages/tools-language/src/ containing array.ts, function.ts, math.ts, and properties.ts

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please consult the source files under packages/tools-language/src/ for the exact function signatures and behavior. The dispatcher at rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.ts shows how each op maps to function calls.

feature1_array_index.json.description: Convert a signed positional offset into a non-negative array index, given an array and an offset. A zero or positive offset is returned unchanged (even when it exceeds the array length). A negative offset counts backward from the end and is resolved to the array length plus the offset, which may itself be negative when the magnitude exceeds the length. The output reports the resolved index value.

feature2_array_addrange.json.description: Append the elements of a source list onto a destination list within an optional range, and report the resulting list. When the source is absent or empty, the destination is returned unchanged. When the destination is absent, a fresh list holding the selected slice of the source is returned instead. The optional start and end bounds select a sub-range of the source; a negative bound counts backward from the end of the source. The output reports the resulting list, or the neutral absent marker when there is no list to return.

feature3_try_invoke.json.description: Invoke a zero-argument operation and capture either its result or the error it raised, returning a two-slot outcome. The first slot holds the produced value and the second is the absent marker when the operation completes normally; on failure the first slot is the absent marker and the second carries the raised error's message. The request selects whether the operation returns a value or raises an error carrying a given message. The output reports both slots on separate lines, in order.

feature4_approx_equal.json.description: Decide whether two numbers are close enough to be treated as equal, given a non-negative tolerance. They are considered equal when the absolute difference between them is strictly less than the tolerance, and not equal otherwise. This is useful for comparing decimal values that cannot be compared with exact equality. The output reports the equality decision.

feature5_1_pick_value.json.description: Pick a single property's value from a source object and return it wrapped in a brand-new single-property object, optionally renaming the property. The picked value is included only when the property is present and its value is not the absent marker; defined falsy values (such as the boolean false, the number zero, the explicit null, and the empty string) are still picked. When an alternate output name is supplied, the returned object uses that name as its key. When nothing is picked, the absent marker is reported instead.

feature5_2_pick_values.json.description: Pick the values for several requested properties from a source object and return them collected into one new object, optionally renaming each property. A property contributes to the result only when it is present in the source and its value is not the absent marker; requested properties that are missing are silently skipped. When alternate output names are supplied, they are used positionally as the keys of the picked values, preserving the order in which the properties were requested. When no requested property yields a value (including when no properties are requested), the absent marker is reported instead.

feature6_1_extend_object.json.description: Merge a set of additional properties into a single existing object in place, and report the resulting object. Every supplied additional property is added to the original object, and the same (now-extended) object is returned. When no additional properties are supplied, the object is reported unchanged. The output reports the resulting object with the original properties first, followed by any added properties.

feature6_2_extend_object_array.json.description: Merge a set of additional properties into every object of a list in place, and report the resulting list. Each element of the list receives all supplied additional properties, and the same (now-extended) list is returned. When no additional properties are supplied, every element is reported unchanged. The output reports the resulting list with each element's original properties first, followed by any added properties.

feature7_has_property.json.description: Determine, for each requested property name, whether that property exists on a given subject. A property exists only when the subject is a non-null object that contains the property; any non-object subject (text, number, boolean, the explicit null, or the absent marker when no subject is provided) yields a negative result for every requested property. The output reports one line per requested property, in the order requested, naming the property and its existence decision.
